summary: Technical debt is a metaphor coined by Ward Cunningham that frames software cruft—deficiencies in internal quality—as a financial obligation where extra effort on features represents interest payments. The metaphor helps teams decide which cruft to address by considering how often code areas are modified; frequently-changed code demands zero tolerance for cruft while stable ugly code can be ignored. Fowler warns against using debt as justification for neglecting quality, noting that teams often 'max out their credit cards' while still delivering late. The best approach is gradual principal repayment: spending extra time on each feature to remove some cruft, naturally focusing effort where it matters most.
key_points: Technical debt interest is only paid when you modify that code, unlike financial debt which accrues over time—crufty but stable code can be safely ignored
Gradual improvement naturally focuses cleanup effort on frequently-modified areas where cruft removal provides the most benefit
Teams who take on debt to speed delivery often end up maxing out all their credit cards but still delivering later than if they'd maintained quality
Since we cannot objectively measure productivity, all debt calculations are estimates with low accuracy
High-activity areas of code need zero-tolerance for cruft because interest payments become cripplingly high
money_quotes: Technical Debt is a metaphor, coined by Ward Cunningham, that frames how to think about dealing with this cruft, thinking of it like a financial debt.

The great benefit of gradual improvement like this is that it naturally means we spend more time on removing cruft in those areas that we modify frequently, which are exactly those areas of the code base where we most need the cruft to be removed.
Teams who do this end up maxing out all their credit cards, but still delivering later than they would have done had they put the effort into higher internal quality.
Taking on debt to speed delivery only works if you stay below the design payoff line of the DesignStaminaHypothesis, and teams hit that line in weeks rather than months.
So crufty but stable areas of code can be left alone. In contrast, areas of high activity need a zero-tolerance attitude to cruft, because the interest payments are cripplingly high.
Since we CannotMeasureProductivity, none of these costs are objectively measurable.
key_insight: The true power of the technical debt metaphor lies not in justifying shortcuts but in providing a framework for prioritizing cleanup—focus ruthlessly on frequently-changed code while leaving stable cruft alone.
